skill.name: mcp-patterns
skill.description: Reference patterns for building Model Context Protocol servers, including tools, resources, authentication, state, and testing examples for Claude Code integrations.
skill.summary: Build MCP servers with practical patterns for tools, resources, authentication, state, and tests.
skill.author: 0xDarkMatter

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command execution findings, but these are Markdown code fences and reference links rather than executable shell behavior. The skill is documentation-oriented and shows no evidence of malicious intent or prompt injection, but several examples involve environment secrets, outbound requests, local state, and a URL-fetching tool pattern that needs security review before reuse.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled URL Fetch Pattern Needs Validation
medium_findings.description: The tool error-handling example passes arguments["url"] into a fetch helper without showing URL validation, allowlisting, or private-network blocking. This is a documentation pattern rather than live code, but copying it directly into an MCP server could create SSRF or unintended data access risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example clearly fetches a URL supplied through tool arguments. It is not executable in the skill itself, but the missing validation is a real risk if used as a server pattern.

medium_findings.title: Credential and Token Examples Require Secret Handling Controls
medium_findings.description: The authentication examples read API keys from environment variables and attach bearer tokens to outbound requests. This is legitimate MCP guidance, but implementations should avoid logging secrets, restrict endpoints, and document required scopes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment and bearer-token usage is explicit and expected for an auth pattern. The risk is contextual because no evidence shows exfiltration or malicious endpoints.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported many Ruby or shell backtick findings, but the referenced lines are Markdown fenced examples and tables. No evidence found of executable shell scripts, command interpolation, or runtime command execution in the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited backticks delimit Markdown code blocks and inline references. The files are documentation and do not contain runnable shell command wrappers.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation References or Placeholders
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to example.com placeholder APIs, an auth placeholder, and official MCP documentation links. No evidence found that the skill contacts these URLs automatically or sends local data to them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in sample code and documentation lists only. There is no execution path in the skill that performs network requests.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Not Supported by Context
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptographic algorithm findings, but the cited MCP and tool registration lines do not contain cryptographic functions or hash operations. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, insecure cipher use, or custom cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the cited lines shows MCP descriptions, schema fields, and tool names. The cryptography label is not semantically supported by the file content.

low_findings.title: Local SQLite State Example Is Not a Bundled Database
low_findings.description: The SQLite finding refers to an example path for storing MCP server state under the user home directory. It is not a bundled database file, but users should avoid storing secrets or sensitive records without encryption and retention controls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The local database pattern is real but appears as sample documentation. Risk depends on what future implementers store in that database.

dangerous_patterns.title: Potential SSRF Pattern in URL Fetch Tool Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The fetch_data example accepts a URL from tool arguments without showing validation or allowlisting. Implementations should validate schemes, hosts, redirects, and private IP ranges before using this pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example uses user-provided URL input. The finding is limited to documentation risk because the skill does not execute the request itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Secret Handling Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples show API keys and bearer tokens being loaded from environment configuration. This is a normal integration pattern, but it requires least-privilege scopes, masking, and endpoint restrictions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive access pattern is explicit, but no malicious use is present. Controls depend on downstream implementations.

content.value_statement: MCP server authors need repeatable patterns for tools, resources, authentication, state, and tests. This skill gives concise reference examples for Claude Code integration work.
actual_capabilities: Shows a minimal Python MCP server structure with tool registration and tool calls.
Documents Claude Desktop configuration examples for Python and uv-based servers.
Provides reference patterns for MCP tools, resources, authentication, state, and tests.
Demonstrates Pydantic validation for tool inputs and async pytest examples.
Lists official MCP documentation and SDK resources for deeper implementation work.
limitations: The examples are templates and are not a complete production server.
Security hardening is incomplete for URL fetching, authorization, and secret handling.
The skill focuses on Python examples and does not provide equivalent Node.js implementations.
It does not validate generated MCP servers or run conformance tests automatically.

use_cases.title: Create a First MCP Server
use_cases.description: Use the basic server and configuration patterns to scaffold a small MCP integration for Claude Code.

use_cases.title: Standardize Integration Patterns
use_cases.description: Apply shared tool, resource, authentication, and state patterns across several internal MCP servers.

use_cases.title: Review MCP Server Designs
use_cases.description: Compare a planned server against common validation, error handling, testing, and state management patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Server
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a small MCP server.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-patterns skill to outline a minimal Python MCP server with one tool, one input field, and Claude Desktop configuration.

prompt_templates.title: Add Tool Validation
prompt_templates.scenario: Improve a tool handler with safer inputs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the tool validation patterns to design input validation for an MCP search tool with query length and result limit constraints.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Resources and State
prompt_templates.scenario: Design resource exposure and local caching.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the resource and state patterns to propose MCP resources, cache behavior, and storage boundaries for a project data server.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an MCP Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the MCP patterns to review my server design for tool validation, resource access, token handling, persistence, tests, and SSRF risk.

output_examples.input: Design a simple MCP server for a project search tool.
output_examples.output: A concise plan with server entry point, search tool contract, validated inputs, Claude Desktop configuration notes, and focused test cases.

output_examples.input: Review an MCP resource design that exposes project settings and item records.
output_examples.output: A review covering resource identifiers, authorization checks, safe serialization, cache behavior, and error handling recommendations.

output_examples.input: Add authentication guidance for an MCP server that calls an external API.
output_examples.output: A security checklist for environment variables, token refresh timing, endpoint restrictions, logging controls, and failure handling.

best_practices: Validate every tool argument before calling external services or local storage.
Keep secrets in environment configuration and mask them in logs, errors, and test output.
Add unit tests for success, empty results, invalid inputs, and upstream service failures.
anti_patterns: Do not fetch arbitrary user-provided URLs without strict validation and allowlists.
Do not expose dynamic resources without checking whether the caller may read each item.
Do not store tokens, API responses, or user data in local caches without retention limits.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps design Python MCP servers with tools, resources, authentication, state handling, and tests.

faq.question: Does it generate a complete production server?
faq.answer: No. It provides reference patterns that still need project-specific implementation and security hardening.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Are the authentication examples safe to copy directly?
faq.answer: They show common patterns, but production use needs scoped tokens, secret masking, and endpoint restrictions.

faq.question: Does it include testing guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes manual async test examples and pytest patterns for MCP tool handlers.

faq.question: What is the main security warning?
faq.answer: Review any tool that accepts URLs or resource identifiers from users before it reaches a network call or data store.
